data.title: Altria: Packs A Stable Punch Amid Macro Unease, 6.5% Dividend Yield
data.description:
The S&P 500’s bull market is set to turn three years old this weekend. Over that stretch, U.S. large caps have nearly doubled on a total return basis. Consumer Staples, however, has been largely left out of the upside move, but not all Staples stocks.

Today, I’m revisiting a major blue-chip winner since early 2024: Altria (NYSE:MO). I had a "Buy" rating on the now $110 billion market cap tobacco industry stock. Like many of its peers, shares are up substantially since my June 2024 analysis—the return, dividends included, is 65%.

I reiterate a "Buy" rating on the stock, which now sports a 6.45% forward dividend yield. The P/E ratio remains modest, while earnings growth should be steady in the years to come. I’ll also note where I see MO heading in the months ahead based on technical trends.

MO Marches Higher As XLP Sputters Since The Bull Market Began In October 2022

Back in July, Altria reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $1.44 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $1.38, while revenue of $6.1 billion, down 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a strong $910 million beat.

Soft sales numbers were largely the result of lower revenue from its smokable products segment, but there were gains in its oral tobacco products segment (NJOY and on! Drove significant gains with a robust 26.5% shipment volume increase YoY). The management team then raised the lower end of its 2025 full-year EPS guidance range.

Oral Tobacco Growth

Shares rallied 3.6% in the session that followed, its best post-earnings performance since October 2024. Ahead of Q3 results due out on Oct. 30, the options market prices in a small 3.4% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the reporting date.

Looking back at the quarter that was, the company kept puffing out solid profit growth despite challenges in the tobacco industry. Altria managed to raise prices without cutting into broader growth, particularly in its nicotine pouch business. That helped to offset the weakness in cigarettes and smokeless-tobacco volume.

MO has long rewarded shareholders, but the management team noted that buyback activity could slow in the back half of 2025 compared to the same period last year. In 1H, over $4 billion was returned to stockholders via dividends and buybacks.

Still, operating income growth of 5.5% was encouraging, and current free cash flow levels appear sound for ongoing share repurchases and the lofty dividend. For the upcoming quarterly report, buyback plans will be interesting, while the margin story will be in focus.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be slow and steady in 2025 and throughout the years. The current 6%-plus rate may ease, while revenues hold steady just above the $20 billion mark. The sell-side, meanwhile, has turned markedly more bullish on Altria’s earnings potential—there have been a high 11 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days compared to just a lone downgrade.

Finally, profitability trends are healthy for the tobacco stock. FCF per share now clocks in at $5.16, which is a 7.7% FCF yield.

Altria: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates since my Q2 assessment. If we assume normalized operating EPS of $5.60 (near the current consensus for FY 2026) and apply the stock’s long-term average 12-13x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $70.

That doesn’t leave much upside, but investors are paid a large dividend to own the stock. A 12.5x P/E is also more than 10 figures cheaper than the S&P 500’s multiple. A new FDA regulatory plan should support a slight valuation premium compared to MO's long-term average.

MO: A Higher P/E Today, But A Still-Strong Yield

Key risks for Altria include, of course, ongoing challenges in the U.S. nicotine industry, along with the potential for crackdowns and tighter regulations from the Trump Administration and Secretary Kennedy.

The company also called out that inflation has been a key threat on the Q2 earnings call. Thus, persistent 3%-plus annual consumer price increases would be problematic. A weakening consumer environment could weigh on margins—listen for clues on that topic in the Q3 report.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q3 2025 earnings date of Thursday, Oct. 30 BMO.

Be on the lookout for an update to the current stock buyback program, as it's set to end on Dec. 31. Altria raised its dividend back in August to $1.06 per share quarterly.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares approaching full value, but with a high free cash flow and dividend yield, the technical situation appears strong. Notice in the chart below that shares are now within earshot of the all-time high of the 2017 high in the upper $70s. The trend since Q1 2024 is obviously higher, and the long-term 200-day moving average is firmly on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the blue measured move upside price target. Based on the height of the 2022 through 2024 drop and recovery, an objective of $76 is in play. On the downside, there is an uptrend support line that is right near the 50dma, so that should offer some support.

Furthermore, a symmetrical triangle apex (orange) has confluence with that zone close to the $60 mark. That would be an opportunity to buy the stock at a better discount to fair value than today’s level. Finally, with a high amount of volume by price under the current level, there should be ample support if we see a dip.

MO: Motoring Higher, 2017 Peak In Play, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on MO, and I am lifting my price target to $70. The discount to fair value is now quite narrow, but the high dividend yield and strong technicals are positive considerations.

data.title: TSMC Heads Into Q3 With Strong Momentum
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage in June Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) stock has jumped by almost 40%, well outpacing the market as the investors have recognized its leadership in the 3nm, 5nm, and 7nm nodes. However, the valuation has not fully reflected its true potential, as the forward P/S ratio is 56% off the 5-year mean.

Leading up to Q3 earnings on October 16, investors are forecasting another robust print as AI demand continues to support 3nm ramp and client dependence grows. Although geopolitical and forex risk remain, TSMC’s yield leadership cements its position as the indispensable foundry of the AI era.

TSMC’s Market Lead in Advanced Nodes Pushes Topline Growth

TSMC has a lead in advanced semiconductor node-tech, mainly in 3nm, 5nm, and 7nm processes. This node lead provides stable stock price growth through capitalizing on the high demand for AI/HPC chips. The trends in revenue acceleration and increasing contribution from advanced nodes (amid AI-demand tailwinds) led to margin and market share expansion. The revenue trends (monthly) for FY25 point out that TSMC's YTD revenue through September hit NT$2,762,964 million with a 36.4% YoY increase (from NT$2,025,846 million in 9M-FY24).

There is a noticeable structural change in this growth. Revenue increased 36.3% year over year to NT$839,254 million in Q1-FY25 from NT$615,644 million in Q1-FY24. Q2-FY25 increased by 38.6% YoY to NT$933,792 million. With monthly breakdowns of July NT$323,166 million, August NT$335,772 million, and September NT$330,980 million, Q3-FY25 totalled NT$989,918 million (US$34,135 million). This performance, which represents a 30.3% YoY increase, exceeded guidance.

Quarterly growth has remained persistently in double digits throughout, averaging well above 35% YoY during 2025. That's significantly above the pre-2023 average of around 20–25%, marking the much steeper expansion phase. Revenue went up to NT$2,263,891 million during 2022 from NT$1,587,415 million during 2021 by 42.6%, but that was fueled by post-pandemic recovery rather than by AI demands.

The topline acceleration in FY24-FY25 was in line with the AI boom. Historically, the topline growth was stabilizing at 15-20% CAGR from FY19-FY23 (2019: NT$1,069,985 million to 2023: NT$2,161,736 million, ~20% CAGR), but now it has jumped to 33.8% in 2024 (full-year NT$2,894,308 million) and projected ~30% for FY25 based on guidance.

In short, TSMC's Q3-FY25 sales beat estimates amid strong AI chip demand, with September revenue up 31.4% YoY. The street is estimating double-digit topline growth to continue in the upcoming years under strong AI demand. The market is forecasting WFE (wafer fab equipment) growth to $122 billion in FY26 as TSMC is capturing market share due to AI accelerators.

However, the consensus-based topline growth is not in line with the same level of growth seen in FY25. It is making TSM stock undervalued in terms of topline valuation.

Fundamentally, TSMC's moat is coming from advanced node capabilities. For instance, in Q2-FY25, 3nm processes contributed 24% of wafer revenue (up from 15% YoY and 22% QoQ), 5nm 36% (stable but high-margin), and 7nm 14% (down from 17% YoY as migration to finer nodes occurs). These Advanced tech nodes (7nm and below) accounted for 74% of total wafer revenue that is up from ~65% in prior quarters. This tech shift is vital as finer nodes enable denser and more efficient chips ideal for AI training/inference. So, TSMC is commanding premium pricing with that.

The premium pricing is boosting margins directly as Q2 actual gross margin hit 58.6% (beating guidance midpoint of 58%), and the operating margin hit 49.6% (beating 47-49%). AI chips from clients like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) (H20, Blackwell) and Huawei (Ascend 910C using TSMC dies) are driving this growth. As per Counterpoint Research, TSMC's foundry market share jumped to 38% in Q2 2025 (from 31% YoY) based on AI demand on 3nm/ packaging like CoWoS.

Overall, these revenue trends point to advanced nodes ramping quickly. For instance, 5nm in Q2-FY22 led to ~21% topline contribution and hit 36% by Q2-FY25. Whereas 3nm led 6% topline in Q3-FY23 and is on a stronger growth path (24% in Q2-FY25). Also, TSMC has a client lock-in ecosystem of chips. How? Nvidia and Intel Corporation (INTC) rely on TSMC for core AI chips, and there are deals like the one with Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) for Tomahawk Ultra.

This client lock-in solidifies TSMC’s moat as clients like Apple (80% portfolio on 3nm) and Qualcomm (2nm launches) depend on TSMC's chip yield superiority (Samsung struggles with 3nm yields). Thus, a higher advanced node mix in the topline may lead to revenue per wafer growth based on a premium for 3nm vs. 7nm. The increasing mix of advanced nodes can continue to boost TSMC’s overall topline despite flat unit volumes.

Geopolitical Drama and Supply Chain Issues Boost Operational Risks

A main downside for TSMC is its continued exposure to AI-chip geopolitics and supply chain issues. Here, the US-China trade war, Trump tariffs, and rare earth dependencies are leading to adverse policy issues like China's rare earth export rules. These export rules are pushing costs higher and possibly compressing margins for TSMC. Further, geopolitical risks are vital in the case of TSMC’s fab as China is tightening rare earth export rules (with a control of 90% on global supply). So, these rules hit hard on wafer fabrication (TSMC's core) cost-wise through price hikes on inputs.

In the past, since 2019, Huawei's bans, TSMC's China revenue has dropped from 16% in Q2-FY24 to 9% in Q2-FY25 that points to forced diversification and lost volume. Similarly, now, the US has revoked TSMC's VEU authorization for the Nanjing facility (effective from December), and it requires licenses for shipments. These types of regulatory shifts can considerably hurt TSMC’s chip capacity on mature nodes and can lead to rerouting of shipments with higher costs.

Additionally, supply chain issues also boost the bottom-line weakness. How? TSMC's fab ecosystem relies on global inputs for raw materials. Huawei's use of TSMC dies and SK Hynix HBM despite bans points to possible circumvention risks and following regulatory scrutiny. Pentagon's MP Materials partnership to offset China's rare earth monopoly makes TSMC's cost exposure larger. For instance, 2020-2021 supply shortages (COVID/China tensions) caused 10%-20% wafer price hikes. But now reversed, here, FY25 capex $38-$42 billion may hit hard if disruptions hit the bottom line.

Finally, Q3 outlook points to forex swings. Q2 actual 31.05 and 29 in Q3 boosted USD topline but compressed margins (every 1% NTD appreciation cuts TSMC’s revenue by 1%). TSMC is raising $10 billion for its offshore unit to hedge, but it can be ineffective against policy shocks. So, geopolitics can drive topline volatility through forex also as China's rules weaken USD/strengthen NTD. As a result, it can erode the export edge.

Forward Earnings Still Attractive Despite the Rally

Even off the back of a 40% rally since June, the valuation for TSMC looks fair when tied to forward earnings. Consensus calls for FY25 EPS of $9.85, putting the forward P/E up at 30.4x, and FY26 EPS of $11.5, bringing the multiple down to 26.1x, suggesting strong YoY growth of ~17% into FY26, well above sector median. Importantly, despite the recent rally, these multiples remain supported by accelerating revenue, expected to reach $120.7 billion in FY25 and $142.1 billion in FY26, reflecting sustained AI and HPC demand.

Compared to its 5-year average forward P/E of ~22.6x, TSM's premium is justified by structural node leadership and client lock-ins. With the stock still carrying a forward P/S ratio 56% below its 5-year average, valuation appears undemanding relative to growth potential. In short, earnings momentum into FY26 provides a cushion for multiple expansion.

Takeaway

Due to demand driven by AI, TSMC has surged since June, but its valuation remains attractive. Its advanced-node leadership and client lock-ins justify a premium, as revenue is expected to accelerate over $140 billion and EPS is expected to grow approximately 17% into FY26. Earnings momentum supports additional upside despite geopolitical risks.

data.title: Mousetraps: 6 High-Yield REITs Risking Dividend Cuts
data.description:
The temptation always exists to reach for high yield as a source of income and a buffer against volatility. But the yield you see is not always the yield you get. Companies can and will cut their dividends at any time, though they generally avoid doing so as long as possible.

Even in the best of times, there are always mousetrap REITs. These companies offer alluringly high dividend yields, but in reality, those yields often turn out to be an illusion. In fact, sometimes the reason the yield is so high is precisely because the company is doing poorly, and shares have sold off, but the dividend cut hasn't happened yet.

I published a similar list last August and another last April. However, the list has changed, and it's always a good idea to check again periodically to make sure your portfolio is free of mousetraps.

The group of Mousetraps I identified in December of 2023 lost (-6.54)% on average in 2024, while the average REIT gained 14.40%. So those Mousetraps underperformed by a whopping 2094 bps (basis points).

Here's how the August 2024 mousetraps fared over the ensuing 12 months:

Ticker Open Close Gain YAP Div. Pd YOC Total Return
GNL $8.30 $ 6.88 (-17.1) % 13.25% $ 0.93 11.20% (-5.9)%
CLPR $4.05 $ 3.44 (-15.1)% 9.38% $0.38 9.38% (-5.7)%
GMRE $ 46.45 $ 33.75 (-27.3)% 9.04% $3.90 8.40% (-18.9)%
DEA $ 33.13 $ 21.82 (-34.1)% 8.00% $ 2.23 6.71% (-27.4)%
HR $ 17.57 $ 16.54 (-5.9)% 7.06% $1.24 7.06% 1.16%
PSTL $ 14.59 $13.70 (-6.1)% 6.58% $0.97 6.61% 0.50%
CCI $113.45 $106.88 (-5.8)% 5.52% $5.76 5.08% (-0.72)%
Average (-15.9)% 8.40% 7.73% (-8.17)%
VNQ $ 91.10 $ 88.92 (-2.4)% 3.57% $3.47 3.81% 1.41%

Source: MarketWatch.com, Seeking Alpha Premium

Total Return = Gain + Yield.

It was an uninspiring 12 months for the VNQ, which managed a total return of just 1.41%, thanks to a (-2.4)% gain in share price and a YOC (yield on cost) of 3.81%. But the Mousetraps fared considerably worse, posting an average total return of (-8.17)%, as share price slid (-15.9)%, almost twice as much as their high 7.73% average YOC paid after their respective cuts. Notably, their YOC (actual yield, or yield on cost) was 67 bps below their YAP (yield at purchase).

Bottom line: The Mousetrap REITs underperformed again, this time by a substantial 958 bps, and likely almost always will. The risk is real and significant.

Dividend Safety: Why it's important

Dividend safety is crucial to the value of a REIT for one simple reason. When a REIT cuts its dividend, COWhand investors dump shares like a load of ripe garbage, and the share price plummets. The mousetrap has sprung, leaving the hapless investor with the worst of both worlds: Reduced income and a dramatic loss in share value, often leaving little recourse but to sell at a loss or else hold for a very long time in hopes of a comeback. Thus, it's vitally important to avoid mousetrap REITs, and if you are holding one, it's prudent to sell it before management cuts the dividend.

Dividend Safety: How to Read the scores

Fortunately, Seeking Alpha Premium supplies investors with a Dividend Safety score, balancing up to 20 data points for each company. According to Seeking Alpha, over a 15-year test period (2010-2024), 70% of companies that earned an F for Dividend Safety cut their dividends, and 41% cut dividends within 12 months after receiving the F rating. Nearly all (93%) of dividend cuts came from companies rated C+ or lower, whereas companies with a Dividend Safety rating of B- or better ran only a 2% risk of a cut, and that risk fell to 1% for companies rated A- or better.

Based on the 40% figure supplied by Seeking Alpha, we would expect 2.8 of last year's seven Mousetraps to have cut their dividends. However, instead of the expected two or three, there were four companies on the list that cut dividends during the 12-month period following publication of the article.

  • GNL cut its dividend by 31%, beginning with the April 2025 payout.
  • GMRE cut its dividend by 28.6%, beginning with the June 2025 payout.
  • DEA cut its dividend by 32.1%, beginning with the May 2025 payout.
  • CCI cut its dividend by 32.1%, beginning with the June 2025 payout.

So, 57% of companies with a Dividend Safety grade of F did cut their dividends over that 12-month period. In addition, although HR did not cut its dividend during the 12-month period in question, it did cut its dividend by 22.6% shortly after, beginning with the August 2025 payout.

By contrast, CLPR kept the dividend unchanged, while PSTL increased its dividend slightly (1%) beginning with February 2025.

Dividend Yield: How high is high enough?

With two-year and 10-year U.S. Treasuries offering investors anywhere from 3.58% to 4.10% at virtually no risk, the question for an income investor is this: How much additional yield do you need to justify owning a risk asset such as a REIT? With the average REIT yielding 3.86%, highly competitive with the no-risk rate, about half of the REITs are not worth owning, unless the underlying stock price is set to rise faster than the market. If you know how to spot and time those, more power to you. Most of us don't.

Every investor will set his or her own yield cut-off. For the purposes of this article, I will define high yield as 5.25% or better. That's at least 115 bps above the no-risk rate.

Mouse Trap List: REITs in the Danger Zone

Yields change on a daily basis, and Dividend Safety grades also change over time, but as I write this, there are 22 equity REITs with yields over 5.25% and dividend safety ratings in the Danger Zone. They're listed below, in descending order by yield.

Company Ticker Yield Div. Safety
Community Healthcare Trust CHCT 12.58% D-
Gladstone Commercial GOOD 9.87% F
Clipper Realty CLPR 9.48% D-
Global Net Lease GNL 9.28% F
One Liberty Properties OLP 8.10% D-
Armada Hoffler AHH 8.02% D
Easterly Government Properties DEA 7.99% D
Modiv Industrial MDV 7.88% D
Brandywine Realty BDN 7.80% F
National Storage Affiliates NSA 7.55% D
Saul Centers BFS 7.48% D-
Alexander's ALX 7.39% F
Braemar Hotels & Resorts BHR 7.38% D-
Omega Healthcare Investors OHI 6.51% D
NexPoint Residential NXRT 6.45% D-
BRT Apartments BRT 6.38% D-
UMH Properties UMH 6.18% D
Highwoods Properties HIW 6.17% D
Gladstone Land LAND 6.07% D-
CBL & Associates Properties CBL 6.01 D-
Medical Properties Trust MPW 5.78% F
Healthcare Realty HR 5.37% F

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium

The remainder of this article will concentrate on the six REITs with Dividend Safety grades of F to take a quick look at why the Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings system considers the risk of a dividend cut so high. Remember, D is for Danger.

Seeking Alpha Premium provides 14 dividend safety-related data points for Brandywine Realty (BDN). Of these, 11 grade out at D or worse. Although the cash dividend payout ratio looks fine (grade C), payout as a percentage of AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) is well over 100%, earning an F, as do the company's astronomical long debt/EBITDA and long debt/assets ratios. Although Brandywine just cut its dividend by nearly 50%, beginning with the next payout, there's still palpable danger of potentially another cut sometime in the next 12 months.

FFO per share for BDN nosedived by (-47)% in 2024, with another (-32)% drop expected in 2025, while share price plummeted (-30.0)% over the TTM (trailing 12 months). Shares now trade at just 6.9x FFO '25.

The Quant system provides 14 data points for Global Net Lease (GNL). Of these, 11 grade out at D+ or worse. The company's exorbitant FFO payout ratios are especially problematic. Long debt/assets is also substantially worse than the sector average. GNL has cut its dividend three times in the past two years and still appears to be at risk of cutting again.

With a 68% debt ratio and 35.7% of its debt held at variable rates, the tempting near-double-digit yield seemingly depends on an immediate and substantial surge in revenues.

Unfortunately, GNL's nosedived (-58.3)% in FFO in 2024 and is on pace to dive another (-50.0)% in 2025. Meanwhile, the share price declined only (-3.41)% TTM, so the bait is working. Shares are selling at 16.0x FFO'25.

Of the 13 data points provided for Alexander's (ALX), five grade out at D or worse. Total debt to equity and to capital are particularly concerning, as are the payout ratios. FFO to total debt is near zero. Meanwhile, the company's long debt to assets and EBITDA earn high marks.

FFO per share for ALX declined (-17.6)% in 2024 and is on pace to decline another (-14.4)% this year, while the company's price per share actually rose by 6.06% TTM. Shares now sell at a slightly frothy 20.1x FFO '25.

Of the 17 data points provided for Gladstone Commercial (GOOD), eight grade at D or worse. All the company's payout ratios are challenging (except AFFO TTM), and long debt/assets is particularly troublesome.

FFO per share for GOOD slumped by (-7.8)% in 2024 but is expected to come in essentially flat (+1.04%) for 2025. Meanwhile, share price has plummeted (-27.06)% TTM, now selling at 8.1x FFO '25.

Of the 15 data points the Quant system supplies for Medical Properties Trust (MPW), 10 grade at D or worse. Although the FFO payout ratios look good, the forward AFFO payout ratio is weak. Meanwhile, long debt/EBITDA, FFO/total debt, and FFO/revenue are abysmal. MPW has already sliced its dividend nearly in half on two occasions since June 2023. That doesn't mean they won't potentially cut again.

FFO per share for MPW nosedived (-53.0)% in 2024 and is on pace to swoon another (-30.0)% this year. Share price has held surprisingly steady at (-3.5)% TTM, now representing 9.6x FFO '25.

For Healthcare Realty (HR), the Quant system provides 15 data points. While only six of them grade out at D or worse, four of them are F's. These include the AFFO and cash dividend payout ratios TTM and interest coverage at just 0.20.

FFO per share for HR slumped by (-7.7)% in 2024 but is expected to remain nearly flat at +1.9% in 2025. Share price has risen by a modest 3.25% TTM, now selling at 11.3x FFO '25.

The Bottom Line

Dividend Safety is only one factor of many to consider when investing. A grade of F on Dividend Safety means there's about a 40% chance of a dividend cut within the next 12 months. However, a Hold case can be made for some of the companies mentioned in this article, even the seven with the worst Dividend Safety ratings. A Buy case can even be made on a few.

The Seeking Alpha Quant system is not necessarily the final word on dividend safety, but the fact that these companies grade out so low in that system should give a sensible investor cause to pause before adding more shares and good reason to seriously consider cutting exposure and reallocating capital.

And remember, the opinion that matters most is yours. Because it's your money.

data.title: Aixtron: Near-Term Outlook Is Negative
data.description:
Investment Summary

I recommend a hold rating on Aixtron (OTCPK:AIXXF) as the near-term headwinds in its core power electronics markets offset what I see as compelling long-term opportunities tied to AI-driven optics and GaN power adoption. While AIXXF leadership in MOCVD technology and strong positioning in optoelectronics are great, order softness and falling backlog indicate that the current downcycle still has room to play out.

Business Overview

AIXXF is a Germany-based semiconductor equipment maker specializing in Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition [MOCVD] and Chemical Vapor Deposition [CVD] systems, both essential for producing advanced compound semiconductors. AIXXF tools enable the manufacturing of next-generation devices across two key markets: power electronics (covering silicon carbide [SiC] and gallium nitride [GaN] applications for EVs, renewable energy, and data center power systems) and optoelectronics (including lasers for AI data communications, 3D sensing, and high-performance LEDs). AIXXF has a global customer base that includes integrated device manufacturers [IDM] and specialized wafer producers.

Pockets of Strength

AIXXF is experiencing very solid momentum in its optoelectronics segment, whose strength is being driven by the explosive surge in data traffic from AI, hyperscale data centers, and 5G infrastructure. I don’t foresee any slowdown in this data traffic for the coming decade as the world continues to digitalize. The result is that network bandwidth needs to double roughly every two years (source: Q2 earnings), and the current optical infrastructure simply cannot support this. As such, the industry is undergoing a shift toward advanced optical technologies such as co-packaged optics [CPO] and photonic integrated circuits [PICs]. Without going into the technicalities (which readers can do so by Googling), in short, these technologies enable faster, more energy-efficient data transmission. And adoption of these technologies directly benefits AIXXF, as its G10-AsP system has become the “tool of record” (words by management from the earnings call) for producing the laser components.

The adoption trends speak for themselves. Management has highlighted strong customer traction, including new engagements with top-tier names like Nokia and expanded orders from a major global customer’s US operations. This clearly signals growing adoption of AIXXF optical solutions. Importantly, AIXXF expects optoelectronics (especially laser-related applications) to account for roughly one-third of total 2025 order intake, and that provides both strong revenue visibility and a solid buffer against cyclical weakness in the other segments.

Looking further out, there is a major growth opportunity for AIXXF exposure to AI data centers' power needs. The way I see it, a data center's power system is like a city's electrical grid. The old way was less efficient. Now, Nvidia (NVDA) is leading a shift to a new, more powerful 800-volt standard called Kyber, set to roll out around 2027. This new architecture relies on advanced materials like GaN because they handle high power much more efficiently than traditional silicon, reducing energy waste. And here is where AIXXF benefits. AIXXF makes the essential epitaxy machines that build these GaN chips, and it dominates this market with a ~90% share. As data centers adopt this new standard to power AI, which I think they will since power is such a big factor in operating data centers, the industry will need a massive number of GaN devices, which would create a significant new wave of demand for AIXXF tools. While this growth is a few years away, it represents a powerful long-term catalyst.

A Period of Digestion and Uncertainty

While the long-term growth story is attractive, the near-term reality for AIXXF is tough. It is currently navigating one of the most pronounced cyclical downturns in its core power electronics markets in recent history. Both the SiC and GaN segments are in what management calls a “longer digestion period,” as customers work through the heavy overcapacity built up during the 2021–2023 investment cycle. Unfortunately, many chipmakers significantly overestimated near-term demand, and that led to a wave of new fab builds that are now sitting underutilized. The situation is most acute in SiC, where management noted that the installed base in China alone could theoretically meet global demand two to three times over.

So, in China, we have seen over the last two, three years a massive build out in silicon carbide wafer production capacity. I think China today has 6-inch wafer production capacities which is probably good enough to serve 2x or 3x the world market demand, yes? So, in 6-inch we have in China a massive, I say truly massive over capacity. There's literally entire factories filled with tools and they're all standing idle and being turned off...we are still in the point where our customers have a decent amount of idle capacity, unused capacities, both on the silicon carbide and in the gallium nitride. Q2 2025 earnings transcript

While Asia provided some cushion earlier in the year, it is just not enough to offset the ongoing pressure. The slowdown is evident in AIXXF order metrics. For 1H 2025, total order intake declined 15% y/y to EUR250.7 million, and in Q2 specifically, the drop got worse, falling to 33% y/y, reaching EUR118.5 million. This directly impacted the backlog, which now stands at EUR284.6 million at the end of Q2, down 29% y/y. As such, while the optoelectronics segment provided some near-term visibility and buffer, the reality is the AIXXF backlog has contracted significantly, and this clearly signals that near-term revenue will remain soft.

Also, while AIXXF remains a dominant player in epitaxy machines, it doesn’t mean competition is not intensifying. In SiC epitaxy, companies like ASM International (OTCQX:ASMIY) are making meaningful inroads, particularly with IDMs seeking diversified supply chains. However, the bigger threat, in my view, comes from China’s aggressive localization push. Beijing’s policy initiatives are explicitly aimed at reducing reliance on imported semiconductor tools, with significant subsidies flowing to domestic players. These Chinese MOCVD and epitaxy toolmakers are catching up technologically. As local tools continue to improve, the risk is that Chinese IDMs will increasingly favor domestic suppliers, putting downward pressure on AIXXF market share and margins.

Valuation

All in all, I think there are certainly areas to be positive about for the AIXXF equity story. The accelerating demand for AI data centers is unlikely to pause anytime soon, and AIXXF is in a very good position to win demand from this. However, the fact is also that orders are down (at the consolidated level), and backlog is down accordingly. The near-term outlook for AIXXF's core business is just not great. It is also unclear when the recovery will happen since a lot of factories are still sitting idle (which means a lot of spare capacity). As such, I think it justifies AIXXF to trade at a discount to history, as it is still in the “down cycle.” At 17.5x NTM PE, I think it's fair since AIXXF traded at an average of 24x over the past 5 years. Multiples should remain range-bound until there is clarity on the timing of recovery.

Conclusion

I recommend a hold rating for AIXXF. While the long-term catalysts, particularly AI data center power demand and the shift toward GaN-based architectures, are strong, the near-term setup remains challenged by weak order trends and rising competitive pressures. The stock is likely to trade sideways until signs of recovery emerge, especially as customers digest excess capacity built in prior years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EUR/USD Steadies After Worst Weekly Drop Of 2025 As Dollar Rally Holds
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​EUR/USD (EUR:USD) is trading near 1.158, steadying after its steepest weekly decline of 2025 as renewed dollar strength and political uncertainty in France weigh on sentiment. The euro has fallen over 1.4% this week, marking its worst performance of the year, with traders favoring the greenback amid heightened global risk aversion and steady U.S. yields.

The pair continues to move within a descending channel, with sellers maintaining control below the key 1.165 barrier.

Technical Setup Confirms Bearish Bias

Technically, the break below 1.165 earlier this week reinforced a downside structure, leaving the pair vulnerable to further losses. EUR/USD price is now testing the 1.154–1.155 zone, which coincides with the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of the summer advance. A sustained move below this region could expose deeper supports at 1.1485 and 1.14, where the broader uptrend line from midyear aligns.

EUR/USD price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

On the upside, initial resistance sits between 1.162 and 1.166, defined by the 20- and 50-period EMAs. Only a decisive close above 1.17 would reverse short-term momentum in favor of buyers. Momentum indicators underline the prevailing bearish sentiment, with the RSI near 34—hovering just above oversold territory—signaling persistent selling pressure but also a potential for near-term stabilization if profit-taking emerges.

Political Uncertainty Weighs On Euro

The euro’s weakness has been compounded by ongoing political turbulence in France. President Emmanuel Macron is expected to name his sixth prime minister in a matter of hours, a reflection of continued instability within the ruling coalition. The uncertainty has further undermined investor confidence at a time when the European economy is already struggling with sluggish growth and diverging monetary expectations.

Across the Atlantic, the U.S. dollar has benefited from a surge in safe-haven demand, bolstered by concerns surrounding Japan’s new leadership and its potential policy implications for the Bank of Japan. Additionally, the ongoing U.S. government shutdown—now entering its second week—has delayed key economic releases but failed to dent demand for the greenback, which continues to act as a preferred refuge during market stress.

Looking ahead, the University of Michigan consumer sentiment index is set for release later today, offering a key gauge of U.S. household resilience amid the fiscal impasse. Stronger data could reinforce the dollar’s dominance, while weaker readings may prompt a brief corrective bounce in the euro. For now, the broader trend remains tilted in favor of the dollar, with EUR/USD likely to stay under pressure unless political and macroeconomic headwinds ease in Europe.

Earlier coverage highlighted 1.154 as a critical support zone for the pair. That level continues to serve as a battleground between buyers and sellers, with a breakdown potentially extending losses toward the 1.148 region and a rebound requiring a firm close above 1.166 to shift momentum back upward.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Snowflake: 25% Rally Hit My Target, Cloud, And AI Growth Story Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
Snowflake Inc. (NYSE:SNOW), a key player in the cloud and data infrastructure space, has been one of the companies that I have had a "Buy" rating on. Since my last report, the stock has rallied more than 25%, reaching my prior price target of $249.85 and outperforming the S&P 500’s 13.7% gain. With that milestone achieved, it is a good time to revisit Snowflake’s valuation, reassess its growth trajectory, and determine whether the stock still offers upside.

The Investment Case For Snowflake

Before reassessing the stock price target for Snowflake, I believe it is good to start by highlighting the investment case and focusing on what Snowflake does and what it also doesn’t do. In my prior report, I provided a detailed discussion on my journey with cloud solutions and how that enabled me to provide a deeper-level insight into the value proposition, as I had to scale my own company and bring it from local storage to personal cloud storage, and after that to a business level, followed by adopting an enterprise solution.

Snowflake likes to portray itself as a company that prevents vendor lock-in. What is meant by that is that you are not bound to either Microsoft Corporation's (MSFT) Azure, Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG)(GOOGL) Google Cloud, or Amazon.com, Inc.’s (AMZN) AWS. That, in my view, however, is only partially true. When creating an account with Snowflake, you have to opt for either an AWS-hosted service or an Azure-hosted service. So, there is lock-in and no ability at this point to store your data on one and run compute on the other. So, the company is multi-cloud but not a cross-cloud solution. Additionally, running vCores can be more expensive on Snowflake since the vCores run on the selected cloud service, and for each duration a vCore runs, the full virtual compute warehouse is being billed even if not all virtual cores are being used. Moreover, the company does not own the hardware; it simply outsources the computing to the cloud services and applies a markup.

Snowflake is not the most cost-efficient solution in many cases. The appeal for Snowflake customers is entirely different. What Snowflake provides is a SaaS or Software as a Service layer that aids with data engineering and analytics. So, it is not a replacement for any of the cloud services but an added layer. Additionally, there is a marketplace called Snowflake Marketplace that allows datasets to be sold in a query-ready state. This allows companies to capitalize on their data by not only using the insights generated but also selling the datasets.

The Expectations For Q3 2025 And Beyond

Snowflake will be reporting its third quarter results on Nov. 26. Analysts are expecting the company to book revenues of $1.18 billion, 25.6% year-over-year growth, and earnings per share of $0.31, marking 55.6% growth. For next year, the company is expected to generate $4.6 billion in sales, marking 27% growth, and EPS of $1.19, marking 43.7% growth. We note that in the years ahead, the company is expected to book 18%-27% sales growth, depending on the year and between 16% and 44% in EPS growth.

The Valuation For Snowflake

The valuation for Snowflake can be somewhat tricky. At the median EV/Sales of 23x for Snowflake, we see that there is 27% upside with a $317.47 price target based on sales for 2026. The median multiple best captures how the company has traded over time. However, in the other two scenarios, we use the current EV/Sales of 20.6x and the 17.7x EV/Sales of my prior report. The second scenario shows 13% upside this year and 42% next year, and the third scenario shows fair valuation currently and 21% for next year. I do believe it makes sense to look at the expectations for next year and use the 17.7x EV/Sales multiple, which is the most conservative multiple, and this would imply 21% upside with a $302.55 price target. If you are more into following the established median, then using scenario one, you would end up with 27% upside. So, right now I am maintaining my "Buy" rating.

Conclusion: Snowflake Stock Remains A Buy

I believe that Snowflake stock remains a "Buy" as we also start looking at the sales expectations for 2027 since the company’s FY26 will end in January. I believe there is enough reason to maintain my "Buy" rating. My only point of concern over the longer term would be that since the company does lease the compute capacity from the cloud providers, it does not have high capital expenditures, but it does have high cost of sales, and eventually it could become interesting for the company to consider running its own hardware. However, that would also mean that it would be adding a fourth option for cloud storage, and the company has to really consider whether it could be a more appealing alternative than the established players in data storage in the cloud. At this point, I believe it is best to have the high cost of sales rather than trying to provide a company-owned storage and compute option.

data.title: GoodRx: Novo Deal And TrumpRx Talks Point To Ebitda/FCF Upside
data.description:
Introduction

GoodRx Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:GDRX) stock popped then dropped following a report that it plans to join TrumpRx.

In August, the company joined Novo Nordisk (NVO) to offer GoodRx customers access to its popular weight loss drug, Wegovy, for $499/month out-of-pocket. This comes as both Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk are working to increase access to their GLP-1s for self-pay patients amid inconsistent insurance coverage. For instance, both pharma companies sell their GLPs directly to consumers for ~$499/month.

There is a clear divergence between what GoodRx stands for (expanding access and helping Americans save on medicines) and these recent developments. In the article that follows, I propose that GoodRx appears poised to seize these opportunities as leverage to improve its fundamentals.

Business Overview

Because this is my first analysis on GoodRx (although I have some existing familiarity), I want to take a moment to describe what they do and how they make money.

GoodRx operates a consumer-focused digital healthcare platform. It aggregates prescription drug prices and provides discount codes. Its core service is free for users. I have used a discount coupon in the past.

How does GoodRx make money doing this? When a consumer uses a GoodRx code at the pharmacy, the PBM pays GoodRx a fee (aka “transaction revenue”) for directing the prescription fill (~70% of revenues). The company also generates revenue from pharma manufacturers (ads and sponsorships of discount programs, ~17% of revenue) and consumer subscriptions (GoodRx Gold fees, ~10% of revenue).

This structure means that economics are mainly volume-driven. And because GoodRx incurs minimal direct costs on each transaction, gross margins are consistently high (>90%).

Recent Trends

With that said, GoodRx revenues have stagnated somewhat. In its Q2 2025 report, revenues increased just 1% Y/Y to $203.1 million. While this was a slight beat of internal guidance (flat QoQ), it missed Street expectations.

The growth was thanks to Pharma Solutions' revenue (ads/point-of-sale programs for pharma), which grew 32% Y/Y to $35 million. As usual, gross margins were high (93.4%). Although this was a slight drop due to the mix (higher telehealth and program costs). GoodRx’s adjusted EBITDA was $69.4 million. Free cash flow was $49.2 million (24% margin). Net income was $12.8 million.

GoodRx’s recent issues can be tied to a material decline in Monthly Active Consumers (MACs). This dropped 14% Y/Y from 6.6M to 5.7M. The company attributes this to retail pharmacy disruptions (closures) and the end of a large partner program. That being said, GoodRx retains a leading share of the U.S. prescription discount market despite formidable competition due to its strong brand and scale. GoodRx’s subscription plans are dropping, too (778k in Q124 → 680k in Q125). This was attributed to the sunset of the Kroger subscription program.

Despite the 14% drop in MAC, GoodRx has been able to somewhat offset this by improving average revenue per user, or ARPU. So, in Q2, prescription revenue fell only 3%. We call this “improved unit economics” in business school. The company is getting a higher take rate per transaction.

Valuation Assumptions

Assuming a base case of 2.5% Y/Y revenue growth and 16% FCF margins (see Appendix for model assumptions), GDRX appears only modestly undervalued.

"Modestly undervalued" checks out with other metrics:

Modeling GDRX on a cash flow basis was unsurprisingly easy. 1) “Easy” in that it required little tinkering, and 2) “unsurprising” because its stock price is essentially unchanged since Fall 2022 (which implies that its growth/margin assumptions are unchanged).

This does not happen often when I model, but margins and growth carry the same weight for GDRX.

In other words, a 1pp improvement in either margin or growth results in a $0.33 stock price improvement. So, for GoodRx, improving growth is just as important as improving its margins.

Finally, I ran a Monte Carlo simulation on my main DCF function to account for some variability among key inputs like growth, beta, and margins.

Uncoincidentally, GDRX's 52-week range ($3.31-$7.11) is near my model’s 5th and 95th percentiles. These percentiles basically tell us what GDRX could trade between (assuming the base case) without becoming unreasonably over- or undervalued.

As for scenario comparison, I compared my base case with a bullish and bearish outcome.

The bearish one (ongoing Rx-Transaction Pressure) speaks for itself. In the following section, I discuss why I believe Novo/TrumpRx Mix Lift can meaningfully impact GDRX’s valuation if the developments materialize in earnings.

Novo/TrumpRx Lift

GoodRx’s Novo Nordisk collaboration is a direct volume catalyst in Pharma Manufacturer Solutions via branded-drug access programs. Recall that this segment grew 32% Y/Y in Q2. It makes good sense, right? Pharma companies like Novo want to increase access to patients. GoodRx’s 5.7M MACs incentivize collaborations. The collaboration could also indirectly lift Prescription Transactions revenue. If GLP-1 seekers discover GoodRx, they may then use traditional coupons for non-GLP-1 generic meds like antihypertensives and statins. I think that if onboarding/retargeting is tight, this could result in a 1-3% MAC tailwind. GDRX’s initial response to the Novo partnership (~30% increase in early morning trading) may have been the market pricing this in. GDRX price soon faded as investor sentiment is poor and likely in a “prove it” mode.

GoodRx’s potential role in TrumpRx.gov (WH-backed DTC portal) is more uncertain (the site hasn’t launched yet, and GoodRx is in talks to participate). GoodRx could help orchestrate access (think eligibility, benefit checks, coupon/voucher generation) and offer manufacturer solutions (traffic acquisition, program enrollment). If GoodRx becomes a preferred “access partner” listed on TrumpRx brand pages, it could meaningfully lift GoodRx’s Pharma Manufacturer Solutions segment. Prescription Transactions could also benefit from a limited direct lift.

But why GoodRx? I think a neutral, multi-branded access hub is attractive to manufacturers and retailers seeking scale. Moreover, GoodRx’s existing counter integrations position it as a reliable bridge.

Growth Levers

In addition to Novo/TrumpRx opportunities, GoodRx has a number of growth and margin levers to pull.

Starting with growth levers:

Lever (Mechanics)

Metric

Latest

Prior

New Users (GoodRx is pursuing new integrations to acquire users more seamlessly)

SBC as % of revenue

10.5% (Q2 ‘25)

13.3% (Q2 ‘24)

Final Thoughts

While Novo/TrumpRx collaborations provide some upside potential, GDRX’s valuation strikes me as appropriate. Despite recent operating leverage (increasing adjusted EBITDA on flat Y/Y revenues), the KPIs (MACs, subscriptions) are concerning. Now, the Novo collaboration can help stabilize these numbers, but if you’re expecting a “turnaround,” you may need to check yourself.

I didn’t touch on it (it is well-known), but competitors like SingleCare, Optum Perks, Amazon Pharmacy (AMZN), and Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug Company are formidable competitors. While many are smaller (SingleCare’s pharmacy network is about half the size of GoodRx’s) or largely target submarkets (Cost Plus only carries ~1,000 generics; focused on mail-order generics), they are, collectively, still taking share from GoodRx.

The takeaway? Novo plus potential TrumpRx don’t “fix” GoodRx’s shrinking user base. They do shift the mix toward higher-margin manufacturer solutions, which should lift near-term EBITDA/FCF. And as I alluded to earlier, core coupon transactions could see a small indirect life as well.

In the coming quarters, I am most interested to see if GLP-1 funnel traffic will result in MAC stabilization. Once this is established, I think there is a lot of upside potential, as evidenced by my scenario comparison. To get ahead of this real possibility, I'd rate GDRX as a speculative "Buy," but this is in the context of a barbell portfolio (with limited allocation).

Appendix

DCF model inputs assumptions:

  • Year 1 Revenue: $798.9 million—the consensus analyst estimates for FY25 and is in line with management guidance.
  • Revenue Growth: 2.5%—based on recent trends and future estimates.
  • FCF Margin: 16%—on a GAAP OCF-CapEx basis; Q/Q swings due to timing of collections; this is a mid-cycle estimate.
  • Beta: 1.23—based on market data; consistent with industry
  • Interest-Bearing Debt, Cash & Equivalents (includes ST investments), and Cost of Debt—based on financial data.

Everything else is standard DCF modeling methodology.

Other pics from GDRX base case:

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoFi Vs. Upstart: Which Fintech To Choose And For Whom
data.description:
Introduction

I decided to make today a comparison of two well-known and popular stocks among retail investors – SoFi Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOFI) and Upstart Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UPST). You might argue there are no grounds to compare these two stocks because they have little in common besides both being a part of the U.S. fintech industry. But I found there was more resemblance, at least until recently. Looking at the graph below, you would see that both stocks were moving almost identically for a very long time. If one rallied, another rallied as well, and the opposite is also true. All of these moves were related to the U.S. economy and industry tailwinds or headwinds. But recently, they went two different ways: SoFi is rallying, while Upstart is plunging.

That divergence is interesting, and I think it says a lot about how investors now view these companies. It also shows how different their realities have become, even if they still technically belong to the same sector. At the end of this article, I would like to answer whether any of these stocks is a compelling buying opportunity and for what type of investor.

Macro Environment

First of all, I have to mention the U.S. economy, as it directly impacts the stock market, especially the financial sector. We are now in one of the strangest macro environments possible. The United States still faces recession risks, and consumer sentiment is still fragile due to unemployment and labor situation, but the Federal Reserve has already started cutting rates. So, we have this very confusing moment where rate cuts should, in theory, support both companies, but a recession would do the exact opposite. There is obviously no way to predict what exactly would happen, but there is a way to adjust to these conditions using your assumptions.

If the economy manages to stabilize without falling into a downturn, both SoFi and Upstart could continue to perform very well (probably better than now) because they will benefit from cheaper funding and a healthier lending environment. But if the Fed ends up cutting rates precisely because the economy is weakening, the effect could be negative for both.

Revenue Growth

If we look at the past few years, SoFi’s growth has been very consistent. Since 2018, its revenue has increased from roughly 300 million dollars to around 3 billion, which means it grew almost tenfold in about seven years. Since January 2023 until today, its revenue has increased by 132%, which was a little slower than before, but it was expected, as the bigger the company is, the harder it is to grow. In the latest quarter, SoFi reported record adjusted net revenue of 858 million dollars, which was up 44% year over year. I have been following SoFi stock for about a year and a half, and to tell you honestly, looking back, I wouldn’t have believed they would be getting to the $1 billion per quarter in revenue territory in 2025. The company is doing many things right, and it doesn't look like the growth will ever stop.

Upstart is different. Its revenue went from about 100 million dollars in 2017 to ~900 million today, which represents a higher compound growth rate, but it hasn’t been steady. The company experienced huge growth during the pandemic when credit conditions were loose, and then revenue collapsed when funding pulled back in 2022. After nearly three years of revenue shrinking, it returned to growth a year ago, and in the latest quarter, the growth is at an impressive 102% year-over-year.

The Business Models

Even though both companies operate in the same financial technology space, their models are almost opposite.

SoFi is now a diversified digital bank. It truly became a “one stop-shop” for people’s finances, as the management had promised it would be. SoFi gets deposits from customers, provides high-yield accounts, originates and sells loans, runs an investment platform, issues credit cards, licenses its technology to other financial institutions, opens trading and crypto platforms, and has huge plans to expand that even further. It has been working incredibly for them, as this way, they can do cross-selling and keep customers inside their ecosystem. It means they can grow without paying for the addition of new users, which is a great skill for a company in a VERY competitive landscape.

Upstart is completely different. It’s technological, and it doesn’t try to be a bank. It uses artificial intelligence to assess credit risk and connects borrowers with lenders. I have a full breakdown of Upstart from May and a SoFi breakdown from March. Upstart wants to be a platform that helps other institutions make better lending decisions without having to put the loans on their balance sheet. SoFi also refinances loans, but not with AI, and another difference is that UPST focuses solely on that, and SoFi doesn’t.

In general, the idea of loan refinancing is brilliant, but the implementation for Upstart has been difficult. When the Fed began raising rates and fears of a recession increased, banks and credit unions stopped buying loans from Upstart’s platform. To keep the business running, Upstart had to fund part of those loans itself, which is exactly what it didn’t want to do, but it didn’t have another choice.

Now that rates are coming down, this pressure is easing. The company originated 2.8 billion dollars in loans last quarter, which was a 154% increase year over year, and reported positive net income of 6 million dollars. That’s an impressive rebound, but I wouldn’t say the dark days are totally behind us. Upstart’s long-term performance depends on whether large lenders will truly adopt its AI-based model instead of the traditional FICO-score-driven approach.

SoFi funds its operations with deposits, and by now, almost 90% of its funding base comes from its own customers, which is impressive for a young fintech. Deposits reached nearly 30 billion dollars in the second quarter, which gives SoFi a significant advantage in cost of capital. The company can either hold loans and earn interest or sell them through its new Loan Platform Business, which already produces around half a billion dollars in annualized, fee-based revenue.

So it’s clear that SoFi is more independent, which makes Upstart a riskier play, but it could also be an opportunity if things move in a positive direction.

Profitability

Upstart operates like a software company, and when it works well, its margins can be spectacular. In the second quarter, its contribution margin was 58%, and its adjusted EBITDA margin was 21%. That’s the power of a platform model. When the fixed costs are covered, every additional loan adds profit immediately.

SoFi’s margins are naturally lower because it is a bank, and banking is a more capital-intensive business. Even so, SoFi’s adjusted EBITDA margin of 29% is impressive for a company that keeps expanding so quickly. It has now been profitable for seven consecutive quarters and continues to grow its tangible book value, which reached over 5.3 billion dollars in the second quarter.

Valuation

The biggest gap for these two stocks appears in valuation, and SoFi is way ahead. SoFi trades much higher than Upstart. Moreover, on most metrics, SoFi is trading close to its most expensive levels in history.

Upstart’s valuation is the complete opposite. Its forward price-to-earnings ratio is about 26, one of the lowest multiples it has ever traded at. The stock was left behind because investors are still cautious about whether the AI-based lending model can scale in a weak macro environment.

If the economy continues to stabilize and credit conditions remain benign, Upstart could have much larger upside simply because expectations are so low. But if macro conditions worsen or consumer delinquencies rise, the fall is likely to be even stronger than for SoFi, because once again, SoFi is more independent.

Conclusion

Both companies represent a modern approach to finance, but they are very different in their core. But if you were looking for a fintech stock, you were probably considering them both. Both are great companies that could have a solid future, but it looks like they are in completely different phases of their development. SoFi looks much better than UPST does right now. At the same time, SoFi hasn’t always looked as appealing as it does right now. Their road has been bumpy as well, and dozens of risks could have ruined this growth trajectory.

Today, SoFi is becoming a digital version of a universal bank. It has deposits, lending, and investing. It also benefits from a clear competitive moat: once customers join, it becomes increasingly easy for SoFi to sell them additional services. The company reported almost 900 million dollars in quarterly revenue, and the momentum is incredibly strong. However, after such an enormous rally, the stock already reflects a lot of the success.

The stock could rerate after the earnings, and speaking of this month (October), earnings (in less than 3 weeks) will probably be a catalyst from today’s level. But still, the valuation leaves little room for mistakes. If you really like the company, you could start a position and DCA it as time goes by. I think if we are talking about a long term of three or more years, this could look like a compelling buying opportunity.

Upstart is more complicated. It’s still small, it’s very volatile, and it's far more dependent on the broader economy. It remains to be seen whether it can perform consistently during different cycles. The recent return to profitability and triple-digit revenue growth are encouraging, and the valuation looks very appealing now. For investors who believe the economy will hold up, Upstart can be a Buy, but it’s a speculative bet, and it totally relies on your assumptions.

data.title: Lyft: A Strategic Partner For Robotaxis (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I view Lyft, Inc. (NASDAQ:LYFT) as one of the key beneficiaries of the autonomous vehicle (AV) evolution or robotaxi. Ride-hailing companies, in my view, are in the sweet spot of an eventual proliferation of AV/robotaxis. Many AV developers and automakers are seeking JVs and partnerships to fast-track robotaxi deployment to monetize the years of R&D spending, as well as build assembly-line AVs. Lyft is a discounted stock with strategic value.

Tensor Robotaxi Simulation

Is a US$200k robotaxi a viable investment for a private investor using the Lyft network? The short answer is yes, with a few basic caveats. The first is that automobile vehicles are legal/regulated in most urban areas. Tensor/VinFast Auto (VFS), the car and AV technology manufacturer, needs a market to be feasible. Once the regulations are in place, a person could then purchase the US$200k robotaxi and hand it over to the Lyft network and wait for monthly checks.

Using Lyft's average trips and fare data (also corroborated by Uber (UBER)) and current take rate, I concluded that yes, the robotaxi owner could earn a 21% return on investment. This factors in US$200k for the car, US$12k a year in insurance, and US$10k for maintenance, with a useful life of 4 years.

Revenue is based on 20 trips per day at US$19 per trip for 350 days a year (15 days for repair). This generates about US$133k a year in gross revenue, with the robotaxi owners keeping 64% and Lyft 36%. If Tensor/Lyft reaches 100k fleet, it could double Lyft EBITDA. However, I would assume that there would be cannibalization, a loss or reduction in privately owned driven cars. Nonetheless, the theoretical productivity of an autonomous taxi fleet plugged into an established ride-hail network could be highly profitable even with an expensive car/infrastructure.

This is why Waymo, the most advanced robotaxi fleet, is partnering with Lyft and Uber. As I discussed in several reports, the ride-hail companies are in the sweet spot of an eventual robotaxi proliferation.

Current Consensus Forecast

The current consensus estimates have seen some uptick in growth and margins, with expectations of revenue growth driven by a combination of active drivers, trips, and take rates. While cash costs are largely increasing at a slower pace, the scale gains translate into higher margins that in turn produce outsized earnings growth and FCF. Lyft has turned GAAP net income positive in 2024 and should be able to generate over US$700 a year in FCF that may be used for buybacks and M&A.

Valuation

What is most attractive about Lyft is the still high valuation discount vs. its growth and also its peers. The consensus price target of US$19 went stale as Lyft engages in M&A, JVs, and partnerships to accelerate growth and position the company in a robotaxi ecosystem. I still see ample upside based on current estimates (ex robotaxi) and value the stock at 0.75x PEG with a US$30 price target for YE26.

Peer Comps

I used consensus data to compare Lyft to peers, competitors, and related companies. Uber is about twice Lyft in terms of a PEG ratio, while Tesla (TSLA), which supposedly will drive its future from the robotaxi (or is the robot), is more expensive. In my view, Lyft has a large target on it; both Tesla and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) would greatly benefit from owning a ride-hailing network.

Risk

The main risk to Lyft is if Uber initiates a price war and cuts fares, which could hurt margin expansion. The other risks include regulations that reduce the ability to operate under the owner/operator business model. An increase in car costs, maintenance, and insurance is also a deterrent to increased drivers/revenue.

Conclusion

I rate LYFT a Strong Buy. This is a value stock with growth dynamics trading at under 0.5x PEG vs 25% EPS growth. It is also an M&A target for developing autonomous vehicle technology that can leverage Lyft's network at a very low entry cost.

data.title: GSK: Compelling Value, No Red Flags Ahead Of Earnings, Blenrep FDA Decision Soon
data.description:
Intro

When I conducted my analysis of Pfizer (PFE) a couple of weeks ago and performed the peer-to-peer relative valuation comparison, I noticed that while other so-called Big Pharmas were priced nearly the same using the one-year forward EV/EBITDA multiple, GSK plc aka GlaxoSmithKline (NYSE:GSK) stood out, as it was priced noticeably lower.

The pharma sector have been trading at higher levels since the Trump/Pfizer deal, alongside gains in GlaxoSmithKline.

While Pfizer and other pharma stocks have witnessed a pullback after the U.S. Administration deal, GSK has performed better, which could be largely attributed to the CEO transition, since its stock had already rallied over 3% before the Pfizer/Trump initiative was announced.

This relative undervaluation gained my attention, as Big Pharma is already priced quite conservatively, as I see it, and finding one at an even lower level raised my level of curiosity. Still remembered myself, there's no such thing as a free lunch. Usually, something is behind the scenes that causes the market to value a company relatively lower than its comparable peers. Finding the exact cause can sometimes be quite challenging, yet worth conducting as today’s business environment is highly dynamic and the narrative driving the undervaluation might just be on the verge of shifting.

Having conducted this analysis, I rate GSK as a Buy. The buy rating applies if the stock is trading at or below $45. If it rallies higher, I prefer to take a wait-and-see mode. However, the FDA action date for the key drug for growth perspective - Blenrep is 23 October 2025, and this may cause significant volatility. Investors should consider keeping their allocations accordingly.

Recent CEO change

At the time of choosing the theme of what to write an analysis on Monday, September 29, I briefly looked at Glaxo's ticker and noticed in the morning newsfeed that GSK's current CEO Emma Walmsley was stepping down after 9 years and will be replaced by the company's Chief Commercial Officer Luke Miels. I also saw that the share price got a nice +3% boost, and since the company's relative undervaluation was freshly on my mind, I decided to delve deeper into the roots of the GSK CEO change to understand why the market likes the dynamics here, and whether this could be the very cause of the stock's favorable valuation?

BioSpace offers its view:

With the departure of GSK CEO Emma Walmsley, the industry loses one of only two female leaders in charge of a top 20 pharma company—the other being Vertex’s Reshma Kewalramani.

Sounds to me we should be falling here, not to rally? As I understand, women at the helm nowadays are seen as positive catalysts, rather than the opposite.

Still, to gain more context, a quick rewind in time, the very same BioSpace summarizes back in 2021:

Elliott Management has been critical of Walmsley, who took over the CEO role in 2017, for her lack of experience with biopharma. Before taking over as CEO, Walmsley’s background with the company was managing GSK’s consumer healthcare division.

Walmsley spent 17 years at L’Oréal before coming to GSK, where she held several managerial positions, including overseeing the company’s consumer products division in China. Although she has surrounded herself with experienced leaders in the industry, such as Chief Scientific Officer and Head of R&D Hal Barron, Elliott Management has disputed her capabilities leading a profitable biopharma company.

For those who may not be aware, Elliott Management is one of the world's most respected activist hedge funds, known for its quite aggressive tactics to relentlessly pursue shareholders’ value. It is deeply engaging with underperforming companies to push major strategic, operational, or leadership changes. One of their most widely known cases is Twitter (now X), where it pushed to oust then-CEO Jack Dorsey.

Seems to me that not only Elliott, but markets broadly haven’t been really supportive of Emma Walmsley's leadership skills. As the saying goes, "numbers don’t lie," and markets tend to show their view through stock prices, which are, after all, just numbers.

As we can see on the GSK 10-year chart, I marked Walmsley's appointment in 2017 with blue, there hasn’t really been a very good relationship between Mr. Market and Emma Walmsley.

As markets look forward, I don’t delve further into the past since the leadership change is happening, and we’ve taken one reason off the table why markets haven’t treated GSK stock favorably. Let’s look into prospects ahead.

Brief Company Overview

While today's GlaxoSmithKline is a combination of many predecessors through mergers, acquisitions, and partnerships, the company itself dates its history back as far as 1715, with founding roots in London. While this long and proud history deserves a lot of attention, I won't rewrite it here; those who are interested can find an excellent overview on their homepage.

GSK today is a multinational company that employs approximately 70,000 people and is headquartered in London. Its main therapeutic areas are:

Recent Earnings

As of 1H of 2025, the revenue $20.18 billion, broken down as follows:

  • Specialty Medicines $8.14 billion 40.3% of total revenue
  • General Medicines $6.57 billion 32.7% of total revenue
  • Vaccines $5.44 billion 27% of total revenue

The main growth drivers were Specialty Medicines, growing +16% YOY:

During the second quarter, regional sales contributions showed that Europe was the fastest-growing area, seeing sales rise by 11%:

The company summarized its recent 2nd quarter performance:

The main double-digit growth drivers were within Specialty Medicines:

In summary, second-quarter earnings were solid, and management was upbeat regarding long-term prospects.

Lack of Blockbusters

Many Big Pharmas have some outstanding bestsellers, like Merck’s Keytruda, which makes up roughly half of the company's total sales. Pfizer has their Covid-19 vaccines franchise made just almost 40% of total revenue recently. It's really good to have these kinds of impressive blockbusters, but on the other hand, it also raises the level of risk as revenue is more concentrated in a few products. If their LOE or some other cause leads to sales disruptions, then it’s a serious headwind for the company.

While some observers point GSK has too few blockbusters, I personally like how their so-called eggs are nicely balanced in different baskets. GSK bestselling HIV products group, based on Dolutegravir, sold $3.48 billion in 1H2025, which made 17.3% of total sales. The second best-selling product was the Shingrix vaccine, which made $2.3 billion in revenue, accounting for 11.1% of total revenues, and third was Trelegy Ellipta, a COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) and asthma drug, bringing in $2 billion, or 9.7% of the company's total sales. All in all, the top three products accounted for 38.1% of GSK's revenue. A fair point to mention, in my view, is that all three bestsellers belong to different categories, which is also a risk-reducing factor.

3rd quarter Earnings Preview

Since GlaxoSmithKline doesn't provide quarterly estimates, we must rely on their full-year estimates, which management revised slightly upward:

In my view, this signals management's strong confidence in their remaining prospects for the current year. As the pressure from the U.S. administration's tariff and MFN initiatives has also eased somewhat, I see no reason to doubt management's projections.

The Valuation

As I pointed out in the intro chapter, Glaxo notably shines out in peer-to-peer comparisons among fellow big pharmas, which indicates that GSK is relatively undervalued. While I don’t know what exactly is calculated inside the SA sector median multiples, since it shows much higher ratios than outright big pharma comparisons (it's probably all pharma sector, including medical devices, smaller biotechs, PBMs, etc.), it also reflects significant undervaluation as GSK Enterprise Value (EV) metrics are all well below the industry average.

It's important in my opinion that while many Big Pharma companies are projected to face substantial revenue gaps due to major Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) events between 2027 and 2029, GSK's revenue is estimated to grow during that same timeframe. Only the year 2030 is projected to show a slight drop in sales.

The major upcoming Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) problem is often blamed for the relatively low valuations of many Big Pharma companies. Given that, we should conclude that GSK might deserve higher multiples, not lower, compared to other big pharmas.

Since analysts are pointing to GSK's lack of exceptional blockbusters, the benefit of having a more differentiative portfolio is clearly reflected in its lower LOE hurdles in my view. This helps to ensure the company doesn't rely too heavily on a few hit drugs and doesn't get in trouble when these lose patent protection.

Additionally, the revenue generated by the GSK vaccine portfolio is not subject to the typical LOE cycles seen with other treatments, given the vaccine's absence of generics.

Furthermore, management consistently projects revenues of at least £40 billion ($53.2 billion) by 2031, signaling that the business is expected to return to growth starting around 2030.

To summarize here, I don’t see the reason why GSK should be valued lower than comparable pharmaceutical companies facing greater LOE risk. On the contrary, as I see it, GSK's lower risk profile deserves a comparatively higher valuation. This is the basis for my Buy rating.

Risks

While Glaxo faces all the typical risks to the pharmaceutical business – well summarized in their earnings announcements—I point out the three I view as most important:

  • CEO Transition: While there is currently no basis to suggest any specific risk here, the change in leadership always carries some.
  • U.S. Administration Initiatives: While immediate risks have eased after Pfizer/White House deal, Trump administration could surprise with further unexpected policy initiatives.
  • Key FDA Approvals: The successful approvals of depemokimab and Blenrep's new indications expected by the end of 2025—estimated to be multibillion-dollar opportunities—are critical for future growth.

Takeaway

While there are notable near-term risks, as the upcoming Blenrep FDA decision is just two weeks away, I view GSK plc stock as a solid investment. The company has a promising and balanced pipeline, a well-diversified portfolio of marketed products, strong financials, and limited Loss of Exclusivity headwinds.

After being in an underdog position and overlooked by the markets for years, I believe the combination of the CEO transition and underlying growth initiatives makes GSK a good choice if one considers investing in the pharmaceutical sector. It’s hard to see explosive returns here, but the attractive dividend yield and balanced risk profile offer a strong argument for more conservative investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
